items.title: Putting the ‘lord’ in ‘landlord’: US churches step up to build housing amid shortage
items.summary:
The ‘Yes in God’s Back Yard’ movement sees churches across the country develop their underutilized land into affordable housing

A parcel of land behind Little Rock AME Zion church in Charlotte, North Carolina, remained mostly empty for nearly a decade before the congregation approached the city with a proposal.

The land sat unused while housing prices climbed and locals were being pushed out of their neighborhoods. So, the church proposed in 2018, why not develop housing there?

items.summary:
Oakland and New York campaigns for $30 minimum wage gain steam as workers battle high costs and pushback

Mark Dorsey, a lifelong East Oakland resident, works two jobs to make ends meet. The 35-year-old Californian relies on manufacturing and service work through temp agencies and tries to work overtime or 10- to 12-hour shifts because “that’s the only way you can see a paycheck that’s worth something”.

items.title: Trump signature to appear on US currency in first for sitting president
items.summary:
Treasurer’s signature to be removed for first time since 1861 in change made to mark US’s 250th anniversary

Donald Trump’s signature will soon appear on US paper currency, the treasury department announced on Thursday.

The move marks the first time a sitting US president’s signature will appear on legal tender. To accommodate this change, the treasurer’s signature will be removed for the first time since 1861.

science.title: Science

sentiment.label: Mixed
sentiment.rationale: Average of 12 per-headline sentiment scores (raw avg +0.208, dampened ×0.6 → +0.1248). This is a coarse signal; interpret as overall tone, not factual positivity/negativity.

items.title: Popular sugar substitute linked to brain damage and stroke risk
items.summary: A widely used sugar substitute found in everything from keto snacks to diet drinks may not be as harmless as it seems. New research shows that erythritol can disrupt brain blood vessel cells, reducing their ability to relax, increasing harmful oxidative stress, and impairing the body’s ability to break down clots. These changes create conditions that could raise stroke risk, even at typical consumption levels.

items.title: After 20 years, scientists finally explain the Crab Pulsar’s strange “zebra stripes”
items.summary: For decades, astronomers have been puzzled by strange “zebra stripe” patterns in radio waves from the Crab Pulsar — bright bands separated by complete darkness. Now, new research suggests the answer lies in a cosmic tug-of-war between gravity and plasma. The pulsar’s plasma spreads light apart, while gravity bends it back together, creating interference patterns that form the striking stripes.

items.title: This quantum computing breakthrough may not be what it seemed
items.summary: A team of physicists set out to test some of the most exciting claims in quantum computing—and found a very different story. Instead of confirming breakthroughs, their careful replication studies revealed that signals once hailed as major advances could actually be explained in simpler ways. Despite the importance of these findings, their work initially struggled to get published, highlighting a deeper issue in science.

items.title: This new therapy turns off pain without opioids or addiction
items.summary: Scientists have developed a new gene therapy that quiets pain at its source in the brain—without the addictive risks of opioids. Using AI to map how pain is processed, they created a targeted “off switch” that mimics morphine’s benefits but skips its dangerous side effects. In early tests, it delivered lasting relief without affecting normal sensations. The discovery could mark a major step toward safer, non-addictive pain treatments.

items.title: Stroke triggers a hidden brain change that looks like rejuvenation
items.summary: After a stroke, the brain may do something surprisingly hopeful—it can “refresh” parts of itself. Researchers analyzing brain scans from over 500 stroke survivors found that while the damaged side of the brain appears to age faster, the opposite, unaffected side can actually look younger. This unexpected shift seems to reflect the brain’s effort to rewire itself, strengthening healthy regions to compensate for lost function.

items.title: New cholesterol guidelines could change when you get tested
items.summary: A major new U.S. cholesterol guideline is shifting the focus toward earlier, more personalized prevention of heart disease. It urges people to start screening sooner—sometimes even in childhood—and highlights the importance of tracking not just LDL (“bad”) cholesterol but also genetic risk factors like lipoprotein(a). A new, more advanced risk calculator now uses broader health data to better predict heart attack and stroke risk over decades.

items.title: Scientists say the evidence is clear: E-cigarettes beat patches and gum in helping smokers quit
items.summary: Nicotine e-cigarettes may be one of the most effective tools yet for quitting smoking, according to a sweeping review of global research. By analyzing findings from 14 major reviews spanning nearly a decade, researchers found consistent, high-quality evidence showing that nicotine vapes outperform traditional methods like patches, gum, and even behavioral support. While some lower-quality studies produced mixed results, the strongest data clearly favored nicotine e-cigarettes.

items.title: This hidden state of water could explain why life exists
items.summary: Scientists have finally found a hidden “critical point” in supercooled water that explains why it behaves so strangely. At this point, two different liquid forms of water merge, triggering powerful fluctuations that affect water even at normal temperatures. The breakthrough was made possible by ultra-fast X-ray lasers that captured water before it froze. This discovery could reshape our understanding of water’s role in nature—and possibly even life itself.

items.title: This new carbon material could make carbon capture far more affordable
items.summary: Scientists have created a new kind of carbon material that could make carbon capture much cheaper and more efficient. By carefully controlling how nitrogen atoms are arranged, they found certain structures capture CO2 better and release it using far less heat. One version works at temperatures below 60 °C, meaning it could run on waste heat instead of costly energy. The discovery offers a powerful new blueprint for next-generation climate technology.

items.title: Scientists discover why your appetite suddenly disappears when you’re sick
items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how your body actually tells your brain to stop eating when you’re sick. In a new study, researchers found that specialized cells in the gut detect parasites and send signals that ultimately trigger the brain to suppress appetite. This process builds over time, explaining why you may feel fine at first but then suddenly lose interest in food as an infection takes hold.

items.title: Scientists discover a hidden system that turns brown fat into a calorie burner
items.summary: Scientists have identified a key biological system that helps brown fat burn energy by building the networks it needs to function. A protein called SLIT3 splits into two parts, with each piece guiding the growth of blood vessels and nerves inside brown fat. These structures allow the tissue to pull in nutrients and rapidly convert them into heat instead of storing them as fat.

items.title: Scientists say we’ve been looking in the wrong place for human origins
items.summary: A fossil ape discovered in northern Egypt is reshaping the story of human evolution. The species, Masripithecus, lived about 17 to 18 million years ago and may sit very close to the ancestor of all modern apes. This finding challenges the long-standing focus on East Africa. Instead, it points to northern Africa and nearby regions as a possible birthplace of apes.

ai_report.key_takeaway: Recent scientific studies reveal significant health risks associated with sugar substitutes, breakthroughs in pain management, and new insights into human evolution and water behavior.
ai_report.summary: Today's science headlines cover a range of topics including health risks linked to erythritol, new findings on the Crab Pulsar, advancements in pain therapy, and insights into human origins. Notably, researchers have identified a new gene therapy for pain relief that avoids opioid addiction, while a fossil discovery in Egypt may alter our understanding of human evolution. Additionally, a breakthrough in understanding water's behavior could reshape scientific perspectives on its role in life.

notable_headlines.headline: Popular sugar substitute linked to brain damage and stroke risk
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: Research indicates erythritol may pose significant health risks, challenging its safety as a common sugar substitute.

notable_headlines.headline: After 20 years, scientists finally explain the Crab Pulsar’s strange “zebra stripes”
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: Understanding the Crab Pulsar's radio wave patterns enhances knowledge of cosmic phenomena and fundamental physics.

notable_headlines.headline: This new therapy turns off pain without opioids or addiction
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: A novel gene therapy could revolutionize pain management by providing relief without the risks associated with opioids.

notable_headlines.headline: New cholesterol guidelines could change when you get tested
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: Updated guidelines emphasize early screening and personalized prevention strategies for heart disease.

notable_headlines.headline: Scientists say the evidence is clear: E-cigarettes beat patches and gum in helping smokers quit
notable_headlines.why_it_matters: This research supports the efficacy of e-cigarettes as a smoking cessation tool, influencing public health strategies.
